messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
VLM inference latency scales with visual token count but most
tokens are redundant; how can we identify and remove or merge
low-information visual tokens at inference without retraining
the encoder?


### Proposed method
We propose an inference-time pipeline that transforms a large set of frozen visual tokens into a compact, text-conditioned memory without changing the vision encoder or retraining. First, run the frozen vision encoder as usual to obtain N patch tokens. Before decoding begins (or after the first 1–2 generated tokens), perform a cheap probing pass that uses the model’s existing cross-attention to accumulate a per-token importance score by summing attention mass to each visual token across layers/heads and weighting by the token’s value-norm. In parallel, build a spatially-regularized similarity graph in the visual feature space (nearest-neighbor links constrained by patch adjacency). This gives two signals: token salience (text-conditioned) and token redundancy (feature-similarity + spatial proximity).

Next, adaptively choose a target visual budget r << N based on a global image-dependency gauge: compute a prompt-dependency measure once every few decoding steps by comparing the next-token distribution with and without the visual prompt (e.g., using a symmetric f-divergence). When dependency is high (e.g., early steps, fine-grained queries), keep a larger budget; as dependency fades during generation, shrink r more aggressively. Given r, prune the lowest-salience tokens while enforcing coverage: retain at least one representative per connected region in the similarity graph via farthest-point sampling in feature space, ensuring small but important objects are not lost. Then merge the remaining tokens into r “supertokens” by attention-aware aggregation: for each cross-attention layer/head, form new keys/values as convex combinations of the original tokens within each cluster, using normalized weights proportional to their accumulated attention mass (fallback to uniform if no mass yet). Replace the visual KV cache with these r per-layer supertoken KV tensors and continue decoding.

For later decoding stages, optionally switch from token clusters to a tiny set of virtual conditioning slots that approximate the effect of all visual tokens. Construct M << r virtual slots per cross-attention layer by solving a per-instance low-rank approximation of the token-space: compute the top-M components of the attention-weighted token covariance for keys and values, and instantiate virtual K,V as these components scaled to match the first and second moments of the original K,V under current attention. Inject these virtual slots as a fixed “visual prefix memory” so subsequent steps attend to a constant, tiny set, eliminating further attention over spatial tokens while preserving conditioning.

To keep quality stable, employ a conservative guardrail: after each pruning/merging event, evaluate the dependency gauge on the next step; if it drops below a user-specified threshold (indicating visual influence was over-removed) or the next-token log-probability degrades beyond a small tolerance, partially roll back by restoring the top-k pruned tokens (by salience) or increasing M. This feedback loop converges to the smallest token budget that maintains the model’s current reliance on the image for the given prompt.

### Rationale
The key to reducing VLM latency is to shrink the cross-attention’s visual KV memory online, but only where the image contributes little to the current next-token distribution. The dependency measure provides an annotation-free, per-step signal of how much the model still needs the image; using it to schedule pruning avoids brittle, one-size-fits-all budgets. Within a given budget, attention-weighted clustering preserves the tokens that matter for the prompt while merging redundant, spatially similar patches, which directly cuts the number of visual keys/values without modifying the encoder.

Finally, once the model’s reliance on the image fades, compressing the visual memory to a handful of virtual conditioning slots replaces many tokens with a compact prefix-style representation. This maintains the conditioning pathway with constant-time cross-attention while avoiding retraining by using per-instance low-rank approximations derived from the model’s own attention statistics.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [CV] ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer (2203.10790)
   problem: Vanilla self-attention has quadratic cost in the number of tokens and uses fixed Q/K/V dimensions tied to the input, which limits context-oriented generalization and produces dispersed, non-object-centric activations.
   method:  Scalable Self-Attention (SSA) introduces two scaling factors to decouple and resize Q, K, and V: a spatial factor rn that reduces the number of tokens (aggregating redundant tokens) and a channel factor rc that expands channel capacity for richer representations. Concretely, f_q scales Q’s channels from C to C·rc, f_k scales K’s spatial dimension from N to N·rn and channels to C·rc, and f_v scales V’s spatial dimension from N to N·rn; these are implemented via convolutions plus linear projections, and output dimensions are restored for residuals. This reduces unnecessary intermediate multiplications and yields a complexity O(N·N·rn·C + N·N·rn·C·rc) that is linear in input size as used, while achieving contiguous, object-sensitive features and maintaining contextual cues.

2. [LLM_NLP] Bad Form: Comparing Context-Based and Form-Based Few-Shot Learning in Distributional Semantic Models (1910.00275)
   problem: Standard few-shot updating with Word2Vec/FastText can distort background embeddings and conflicts with aggressive learning rates; additive/ALC/FCM-style context aggregation treats all tokens uniformly, underusing informative context structure.
   method:  Propose selective Word2Vec and selective FastText that update only the target (test) word vector and prevent test words from appearing as negative samples, avoiding background drift and enabling higher-risk learning when beneficial. Further, integrate Word2Vec principles into additive and A La Carte (and thus FCM’s context part): apply sampled window-based distance weights, subsampling weights for frequent words, and adjust sums by subtracting the expected negative sample vector before weighting. With tuned hyperparameters, these simple modifications consistently improve baselines and advanced models across tasks, contributing to new state of the art results on multiple evaluations.

3. [SPEECH] End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering (2511.09282)
   problem: Training the non-autoregressive ASR within the retriever is brittle—recognition errors degrade the learned token distributions and downstream retrieval quality; improving the decoder’s context modeling and stability is needed.
   method:  CLSR introduces a sampler for training: after a first-pass decode, it compares predicted tokens with ground truth, and selectively replaces embeddings of misrecognized tokens by their true text embeddings at a mixing ratio λ to form semantic features for a second decoding pass. This stabilizes token distributions used by the VQ adaptor and improves both WER and retrieval recall. Ablations show that removing the VQ quantizer severely harms retrieval, while enabling the sampler improves ASR and retrieval metrics (e.g., on Spoken-SQuAD, WER decreases and R@k increases).

4. [MULTIMODAL] HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks (2203.03878)
   problem: Equipping text-pretrained LMs with vision-and-language capabilities efficiently—without appending image tokens that consume input length or full-model fine-tuning—requires a new mechanism to inject visual information with few parameters.
   method:  VL-HyperPELT treats image features as inputs to hypernetworks rather than as sequence tokens: CLIP-extracted visual features are projected into visual hyper-embeddings, which drive visual hypernetworks to generate visual-specific prefix vectors for attention and adapter weights for FFN. These are combined with task-specific outputs (concatenating prefix vectors; parallel adapters) to modulate the frozen T5 while preserving the original text input length. This approach achieves comparable or better performance than VL-Adapter with fewer trainable parameters (6.62% vs 7.16%) and yields stronger few-shot transfer on OKVQA and SNLI-VE.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding (2403.14003)

2. TopV: Compatible Token Pruning with Inference Time Optimization for Fast and Low-Memory Multimodal Vision Language Model (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2025, cites: 37)
   Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demand substantial computational resources during inference, largely due to the extensive visual input tokens for representing visual information. Previous studies have noted that visual tokens tend to receive less attention than text tokens, suggesting their lower importance during inference and potential for pruning. However, their methods encounter several challenges: reliance on greedy heuristic criteria for token importance and incompatibility with FlashAttention and KV cache. To address these issues, we introduce TopV, a compatible TOken Pruning with inference Time Optimization for fast and low-memory VLM, achieving efficient pruning without additional training or fine-tuning. Instead of relying on attention scores, we formulate token pruning as an optimization problem, accurately identifying important visual tokens while remaining compatible with FlashAttention. Additionally, since we only perform this pruning once during the prefilling stage, it effectively reduces KV cache size. Our optimization framework incorporates a visual-aware cost function considering factors such as Feature Similarity, Relative Spatial Distance, and Absolute Central Distance, to measure the importance of each source visual token, enabling effective pruning of low-importance tokens. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms previous token pruning methods, validating the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach.

3. SparseVLM: Visual Token Sparsification for Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference (International Conference on Machine Learning 2024, cites: 308)
   In vision-language models (VLMs), visual tokens usually bear a significant amount of computational overhead despite sparsity of information in them when compared to text tokens. To address this, most existing methods learn a network to prune redundant visual tokens using certain training data. Differently, we propose a text-guided training-free token optimization mechanism dubbed SparseVLM that eliminates the need of extra parameters or fine-tuning costs. Given that visual tokens complement text tokens in VLM's linguistic reasoning, we select relevant text tokens to rate the significance of visual tokens using self-attention matrices and, then, prune visual tokens using the proposed strategy to maximize sparsity while retaining information. In particular, we introduce a rank-based strategy to adaptively determine the sparsification ratio for each layer, alongside a token recycling method that compresses pruned tokens into more compact representations. Experimental results show that SparseVLM increases the efficiency of various VLMs in a number of image and video understanding tasks. For example, LLaVA when equipped with SparseVLM achieves 54% reduction in FLOPs, 37% decrease in CUDA latency while maintaining 97% of its original accuracy. Our code is available at https://github.com/Gumpest/SparseVLMs.

4. An Energy-Efficient Vision Language Model Inference with Importance-aware Token Pruning (Asia Pacific Conference on Circuits and Systems 2025, cites: 0)
   Multimodal large language models (MM LLMs) have shown significant advancements in visual reasoning and image-text understanding, but their high computational and energy demands limit their deployment on mobile and edge devices. In this paper, we propose two optimization methods for Mo-bileVLMv2, a lightweight Vision Language Model(VLM), to reduce its computation and energy consumption without sacrificing noticeable performance. First, we introduce token-importance-based image pruning, which leverages attention scores from the Vision Encoder's CLS token to eliminate redundant image tokens. This method reduces the token count by 40%, with only a 1.3% accuracy decrease. Second, we propose sliding window attention for reducing key-value(KV) cache size, storing and loading the cache only for the start token and the most recent text tokens while omitting less critical ones from the attention map. Combined with the first method, this technique reduces the KV cache size by an additional 19.1%, with a 2.2% accuracy loss. Combined, these methods achieve a 51.9% reduction in energy consumption at a modest 3.5% accuracy loss, enabling energy-efficient and faster inference on mobile and edge devices. These optimizations pave the way for practical applications of multimodal models in resource-constrained environments, enhancing accessibility and user experience.

5. A Survey on Visual Token Compression for Efficient Vision-Language Models (2025 5th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Neural Networks (AANN) 2025, cites: 1)
   Driven by the continuous development of multimodal large models, the number of visual tokens has rapidly increased, which in turn raises memory consumption and computational burdens, limiting the widespread adoption of these models in resource-constrained environments. Visual token compression plays a significant role in the development and application of efficient vision-language models, particularly in enhancing computational efficiency and reducing inference costs. Existing visual token compression techniques primarily reduce the number of visual tokens through merging, pruning, and hybrid strategies, thereby reducing computational costs while improving efficiency without sacrificing model performance. Despite the many studies proposing different compression methods, a systematic review of these methods is still lacking. To address this gap, this paper provides a comprehensive review of visual token compression techniques for vision-language models, focusing on the analysis of compression strategies, classification of methods, and their applications and effectiveness in multimodal tasks. The paper first introduces the impact of visual tokens on computational costs and outlines the motivations for visual token compression. It then categorizes existing compression methods into three main types: merging strategies, pruning strategies, and hybrid strategies, and provides a detailed summary and comparative analysis of the basic principles, advantages, and limitations of each method. Through experiments on several mainstream multimodal benchmark datasets, the paper demonstrates the balance between compression rate and inference performance achieved by different compression methods and discusses the future development directions and challenges.

6. Libra-Merging: Importance-Redundancy and Pruning-Merging Trade-Off for Acceleration Plug-In in Large Vision-Language Model (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2025, cites: 2)
   Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have achieved significant progress in recent years. However, the expensive inference cost limits the realistic deployment of LVLMs. Some works find that visual tokens are redundant and compress tokens to reduce the inference cost. These works identify important non-redundant tokens as target tokens, then prune the remaining tokens (non-target tokens) or merge them into target tokens. However, target token identification faces the token importance-redundancy dilemma. Besides, token merging and pruning face a dilemma between disrupting target token information and losing non-target token information. To solve these problems, we propose a novel visual token compression scheme, named Libra-Merging. In target token identification, Libra-Merging selects the most important tokens from spatially discrete intervals, achieving a more robust token importance-redundancy trade-off than relying on a hyper-parameter. In token compression, when non-target tokens are dissimilar to target tokens, Libra-Merging does not merge them into the target tokens, thus avoiding disrupting target token information. Meanwhile, Libra-Merging condenses these non-target tokens into an information compensation token to prevent losing important non-target token information. Our method can serve as a plug-in for diverse LVLMs, and extensive experimental results demonstrate its effectiveness. The code will be publicly available at https://github.com/longrongyang/Libra-Merging.

7. Multi-Cue Adaptive Visual Token Pruning for Large Vision-Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 3)
   As the computational needs of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) increase, visual token pruning has proven effective in improving inference speed and memory efficiency. Traditional pruning methods in LVLMs predominantly focus on attention scores to determine token relevance, overlooking critical aspects such as spatial position and token similarity. To this end, we introduce AdaptPrune, a novel plug-and-play training-free pruning method that builds on conventional attention-based pruning by integrating spatial distance and token similarity with an adaptive NMS approach. Our method is based on several observed phenomena in large models: the positional bias in the model's image attention and the redundancy of token information ignored by previous approaches. By integrating attention, spatial, and similarity information, our approach ensures a comprehensive evaluation of token importance and substantially refines the pruning decisions. Our method has been extensively tested across various LVLMs and benchmarks, confirming its robustness and adaptability. The results demonstrate that AdaptPrune consistently outperforms existing methods across various pruning ratios. Code is available at https://github.com/bzluan/AdaptPrune.

8. VLA-Pruner: Temporal-Aware Dual-Level Visual Token Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Inference (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 4)
   Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown great promise for embodied AI, yet the heavy computational cost of processing continuous visual streams severely limits their real-time deployment. Token pruning (keeping salient visual tokens and dropping redundant ones) has emerged as an effective approach for accelerating Vision-Language Models (VLMs), offering a solution for efficient VLA. However, these VLM-specific token pruning methods select tokens based solely on semantic salience metrics (e.g., prefill attention), while overlooking the VLA's intrinsic dual-system nature of high-level semantic understanding and low-level action execution. Consequently, these methods bias token retention toward semantic cues, discard critical information for action generation, and significantly degrade VLA performance. To bridge this gap, we propose VLA-Pruner, a versatile plug-and-play VLA-specific token prune method that aligns with the dual-system nature of VLA models and exploits the temporal continuity in robot manipulation. Specifically, VLA-Pruner adopts a dual-level importance criterion for visual token retention: vision-language prefill attention for semantic-level relevance and action decode attention, estimated via temporal smoothing, for action-level importance. Based on this criterion, VLA-Pruner proposes a novel dual-level token selection strategy that adaptively preserves a compact, informative set of visual tokens for both semantic understanding and action execution under given compute budget. Experiments show that VLA-Pruner achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple VLA architectures and diverse robotic tasks.

9. RedVTP: Training-Free Acceleration of Diffusion Vision-Language Models Inference via Masked Token-Guided Visual Token Pruning (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in multimodal reasoning and generation, yet their high computational demands remain a major challenge. Diffusion Vision-Language Models (DVLMs) are particularly attractive because they enable parallel token decoding, but the large number of visual tokens still significantly hinders their inference efficiency. While visual token pruning has been extensively studied for autoregressive VLMs (AVLMs), it remains largely unexplored for DVLMs. In this work, we propose RedVTP, a response-driven visual token pruning strategy that leverages the inference dynamics of DVLMs. Our method estimates visual token importance using attention from the masked response tokens. Based on the observation that these importance scores remain consistent across steps, RedVTP prunes the less important visual tokens from the masked tokens after the first inference step, thereby maximizing inference efficiency. Experiments show that RedVTP improves token generation throughput of LLaDA-V and LaViDa by up to 186% and 28.05%, respectively, and reduces inference latency by up to 64.97% and 21.87%, without compromising-and in some cases improving-accuracy.

   Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved impressive performance on multimodal reasoning tasks such as visual question answering, image captioning and so on, but their inference cost remains a significant challenge due to the large number of vision tokens processed during the prefill stage. 1. VGS-Decoding: Visual Grounding Score Guided Decoding for Hallucination Mitigation in Medical VLMs ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Medical Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often hallucinate by generating responses based on language priors rather than visual evidence, posing risks in clinical applications. We propose Visual Grounding Score Guided Decoding (VGS-Decoding), a training-free method to mitigate hallucinations during inference. Our key insight is that hallucinated tokens maintain or increase their probability when visual information is degraded, while visually grounded tokens decrease in probability. We introduce the Visual Grounding Score (VGS), which measures each token's visual dependency by comparing distributions from original and distorted images. During decoding, we reweight probabilities by amplifying visually grounded tokens while suppressing hallucinations. Unlike fixed-weight contrastive methods, VGS-Decoding provides per-token adaptive control. Experiments on MIMIC-Diff-VQA and VQA-RAD across LLaVA-Med, CheXagent, and MedGemma demonstrate consistent improvements, with up to +9.12% overall gain and $+8.98\%$ in open-ended recall, while introducing only $2\times$ inference overhead and no additional training, making it practical for clinical deployment. Upon acceptance, code will be released publicly to facilitate reproducibility.

2. Beyond Static Cropping: Layer-Adaptive Visual Localization and Decoding Enhancement (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have advanced rapidly by aligning visual patches with the text embedding space, but a fixed visual-token budget forces images to be resized to a uniform pretraining resolution, often erasing fine-grained details and causing hallucinations via over-reliance on language priors. Recent attention-guided enhancement (e.g., cropping or region-focused attention allocation) alleviates this, yet it commonly hinges on a static"magic layer"empirically chosen on simple recognition benchmarks and thus may not transfer to complex reasoning tasks. In contrast to this static assumption, we propose a dynamic perspective on visual grounding. Through a layer-wise sensitivity analysis, we demonstrate that visual grounding is a dynamic process: while simple object recognition tasks rely on middle layers, complex visual search and reasoning tasks require visual information to be reactivated at deeper layers. Based on this observation, we introduce Visual Activation by Query (VAQ), a metric that identifies the layer whose attention map is most relevant to query-specific visual grounding by measuring attention sensitivity to the input query. Building on VAQ, we further propose LASER (Layer-adaptive Attention-guided Selective visual and decoding Enhancement for Reasoning), a training-free inference procedure that adaptively selects task-appropriate layers for visual localization and question answering. Experiments across diverse VQA benchmarks show that LASER significantly improves VQA accuracy across tasks with varying levels of complexity.

3. SAGE: Sink-Aware Grounded Decoding for Multimodal Hallucination Mitigation ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Large vision-language models (VLMs) frequently suffer from hallucinations, generating content that is inconsistent with visual inputs. Existing methods typically address this problem through post-hoc filtering, additional training objectives, or external verification, but they do not intervene during the decoding process when hallucinations arise. In this work, we introduce SAGE, a Sink-Aware Grounded Decoding framework that mitigates hallucinations by dynamically modulating self-attention during generation. Hallucinations are strongly correlated with attention sink tokens - punctuation or function tokens that accumulate disproportionate attention despite carrying limited semantic content. SAGE leverages these tokens as anchors to monitor grounding reliability in real time. At each sink trigger, the method extracts semantic concepts from the generated sequence, estimates their visual grounding using both self-attention maps and gradient-based attribution, and measures their spatial agreement. Based on this signal, self-attention distributions are adaptively sharpened or broadened to reinforce grounded regions or suppress unreliable ones. Extensive experiments across diverse hallucination benchmarks demonstrate that SAGE consistently outperforms existing decoding strategies, achieving substantial reductions in hallucination while preserving descriptive coverage, without requiring model retraining or architectural modifications. Our method achieves an average relative improvement of 10.65% on MSCOCO and 7.19% on AMBER across diverse VLM architectures, demonstrating consistent gains in hallucination mitigation.

4. Instruction-Evidence Contrastive Dual-Stream Decoding for Grounded Vision-Language Reasoning ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit strong performance in instruction following and open-ended vision-language reasoning, yet they frequently generate fluent outputs that are weakly grounded in visual evidence. Prior works have shown that instruction prompting further worsens this issue by amplifying language priors, especially when the visual signal is uncertain or ambiguous. To address this challenge, we propose a decoding framework that explicitly balances linguistic informativeness and visual faithfulness during generation. Our method, Instruction-Evidence Contrastive Dual-Stream Decoding (IECD2), maintains two parallel probability distributions of tokens at each decoding step: an instruction-driven stream that promotes expressive and informative responses, and an evidence-driven stream that enforces strict grounding in the image. These two streams are adaptively fused using a symmetric KL-based contrast-based gate, which suppresses tokens favored by language priors but unsupported by visual evidence, while preserving them when both distributions agree. We evaluate IECD2 on multiple datasets spanning various generative vision-language reasoning tasks such as captioning and visual question answering, including POPE, MME, VQAv2, AMBER, MS-COCO, and LLaVA-Bench. IECD2 demonstrates consistent improvements in task accuracy and reasoning performance, alongside a substantial reduction in hallucination across all evaluation metrics compared to state-of-the-art decoding approaches.

1. VL-Cache: Sparsity and Modality-Aware KV Cache Compression for Vision-Language Model Inference Acceleration (International Conference on Learning Representations 2024, cites: 29)
   Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance across a versatile set of tasks. A key challenge in accelerating VLMs is storing and accessing the large Key-Value (KV) cache that encodes long visual contexts, such as images or videos. While existing KV cache compression methods are effective for Large Language Models (LLMs), directly migrating them to VLMs yields suboptimal accuracy and speedup. To bridge the gap, we propose VL-Cache, a novel KV cache compression recipe tailored for accelerating VLM inference. In this paper, we first investigate the unique sparsity pattern of VLM attention by distinguishing visual and text tokens in prefill and decoding phases. Based on these observations, we introduce a layer-adaptive sparsity-aware cache budget allocation method that effectively distributes the limited cache budget across different layers, further reducing KV cache size without compromising accuracy. Additionally, we develop a modality-aware token scoring policy to better evaluate the token importance. Empirical results on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that retaining only 10% of KV cache achieves accuracy comparable to that with full cache. In a speed benchmark, our method accelerates end-to-end latency of generating 100 tokens by up to 2.33x and speeds up decoding by up to 7.08x, while reducing the memory footprint of KV cache in GPU by 90%.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
VLM inference latency scales with visual token count but most
tokens are redundant; how can we identify and remove or merge
low-information visual tokens at inference without retraining
the encoder?


### Proposed method
We propose an inference-time pipeline that transforms a large set of frozen visual tokens into a compact, text-conditioned memory without changing the vision encoder or retraining. First, run the frozen vision encoder as usual to obtain N patch tokens. Before decoding begins (or after the first 1–2 generated tokens), perform a cheap probing pass that uses the model’s existing cross-attention to accumulate a per-token importance score by summing attention mass to each visual token across layers/heads and weighting by the token’s value-norm. In parallel, build a spatially-regularized similarity graph in the visual feature space (nearest-neighbor links constrained by patch adjacency). This gives two signals: token salience (text-conditioned) and token redundancy (feature-similarity + spatial proximity).

Next, adaptively choose a target visual budget r << N based on a global image-dependency gauge: compute a prompt-dependency measure once every few decoding steps by comparing the next-token distribution with and without the visual prompt (e.g., using a symmetric f-divergence). When dependency is high (e.g., early steps, fine-grained queries), keep a larger budget; as dependency fades during generation, shrink r more aggressively. Given r, prune the lowest-salience tokens while enforcing coverage: retain at least one representative per connected region in the similarity graph via farthest-point sampling in feature space, ensuring small but important objects are not lost. Then merge the remaining tokens into r “supertokens” by attention-aware aggregation: for each cross-attention layer/head, form new keys/values as convex combinations of the original tokens within each cluster, using normalized weights proportional to their accumulated attention mass (fallback to uniform if no mass yet). Replace the visual KV cache with these r per-layer supertoken KV tensors and continue decoding.

For later decoding stages, optionally switch from token clusters to a tiny set of virtual conditioning slots that approximate the effect of all visual tokens. Construct M << r virtual slots per cross-attention layer by solving a per-instance low-rank approximation of the token-space: compute the top-M components of the attention-weighted token covariance for keys and values, and instantiate virtual K,V as these components scaled to match the first and second moments of the original K,V under current attention. Inject these virtual slots as a fixed “visual prefix memory” so subsequent steps attend to a constant, tiny set, eliminating further attention over spatial tokens while preserving conditioning.

To keep quality stable, employ a conservative guardrail: after each pruning/merging event, evaluate the dependency gauge on the next step; if it drops below a user-specified threshold (indicating visual influence was over-removed) or the next-token log-probability degrades beyond a small tolerance, partially roll back by restoring the top-k pruned tokens (by salience) or increasing M. This feedback loop converges to the smallest token budget that maintains the model’s current reliance on the image for the given prompt.

### Rationale
The key to reducing VLM latency is to shrink the cross-attention’s visual KV memory online, but only where the image contributes little to the current next-token distribution. The dependency measure provides an annotation-free, per-step signal of how much the model still needs the image; using it to schedule pruning avoids brittle, one-size-fits-all budgets. Within a given budget, attention-weighted clustering preserves the tokens that matter for the prompt while merging redundant, spatially similar patches, which directly cuts the number of visual keys/values without modifying the encoder.

Finally, once the model’s reliance on the image fades, compressing the visual memory to a handful of virtual conditioning slots replaces many tokens with a compact prefix-style representation. This maintains the conditioning pathway with constant-time cross-attention while avoiding retraining by using per-instance low-rank approximations derived from the model’s own attention statistics.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [CV] ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer (2203.10790)
   problem: Vanilla self-attention has quadratic cost in the number of tokens and uses fixed Q/K/V dimensions tied to the input, which limits context-oriented generalization and produces dispersed, non-object-centric activations.
   method:  Scalable Self-Attention (SSA) introduces two scaling factors to decouple and resize Q, K, and V: a spatial factor rn that reduces the number of tokens (aggregating redundant tokens) and a channel factor rc that expands channel capacity for richer representations. Concretely, f_q scales Q’s channels from C to C·rc, f_k scales K’s spatial dimension from N to N·rn and channels to C·rc, and f_v scales V’s spatial dimension from N to N·rn; these are implemented via convolutions plus linear projections, and output dimensions are restored for residuals. This reduces unnecessary intermediate multiplications and yields a complexity O(N·N·rn·C + N·N·rn·C·rc) that is linear in input size as used, while achieving contiguous, object-sensitive features and maintaining contextual cues.

2. [LLM_NLP] Bad Form: Comparing Context-Based and Form-Based Few-Shot Learning in Distributional Semantic Models (1910.00275)
   problem: Standard few-shot updating with Word2Vec/FastText can distort background embeddings and conflicts with aggressive learning rates; additive/ALC/FCM-style context aggregation treats all tokens uniformly, underusing informative context structure.
   method:  Propose selective Word2Vec and selective FastText that update only the target (test) word vector and prevent test words from appearing as negative samples, avoiding background drift and enabling higher-risk learning when beneficial. Further, integrate Word2Vec principles into additive and A La Carte (and thus FCM’s context part): apply sampled window-based distance weights, subsampling weights for frequent words, and adjust sums by subtracting the expected negative sample vector before weighting. With tuned hyperparameters, these simple modifications consistently improve baselines and advanced models across tasks, contributing to new state of the art results on multiple evaluations.

3. [SPEECH] End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering (2511.09282)
   problem: Training the non-autoregressive ASR within the retriever is brittle—recognition errors degrade the learned token distributions and downstream retrieval quality; improving the decoder’s context modeling and stability is needed.
   method:  CLSR introduces a sampler for training: after a first-pass decode, it compares predicted tokens with ground truth, and selectively replaces embeddings of misrecognized tokens by their true text embeddings at a mixing ratio λ to form semantic features for a second decoding pass. This stabilizes token distributions used by the VQ adaptor and improves both WER and retrieval recall. Ablations show that removing the VQ quantizer severely harms retrieval, while enabling the sampler improves ASR and retrieval metrics (e.g., on Spoken-SQuAD, WER decreases and R@k increases).

4. [MULTIMODAL] HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks (2203.03878)
   problem: Equipping text-pretrained LMs with vision-and-language capabilities efficiently—without appending image tokens that consume input length or full-model fine-tuning—requires a new mechanism to inject visual information with few parameters.
   method:  VL-HyperPELT treats image features as inputs to hypernetworks rather than as sequence tokens: CLIP-extracted visual features are projected into visual hyper-embeddings, which drive visual hypernetworks to generate visual-specific prefix vectors for attention and adapter weights for FFN. These are combined with task-specific outputs (concatenating prefix vectors; parallel adapters) to modulate the frozen T5 while preserving the original text input length. This approach achieves comparable or better performance than VL-Adapter with fewer trainable parameters (6.62% vs 7.16%) and yields stronger few-shot transfer on OKVQA and SNLI-VE.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding (2403.14003)
   problem: Autoregressive vision-language models (VLMs) often hallucinate objects or attributes because the model’s reliance on the visual prompt fades as more tokens are generated, and there is no principled, annotation-free way to quantify this grounding loss.
   method:  Introduce a visual Prompt Dependency Measure (PDM) that compares the conditioned distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x, c) to the unconditioned language-only distribution p(y_t | y_<t, x) using distances such as the Hellinger metric (PDM-H). This quantifies how much a token depends on the image versus the language prior. Empirically, PDM decreases with token position (conditioning dilution/fading memory), and lower PDM correlates with increased hallucinated objects in captions, while also identifying ‘contextual pressure’ cases (e.g., function words) where low PDM does not imply hallucination.

6. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Standard VLP training has underutilized techniques like whole word masking and image augmentation, partly due to reliance on cached detector features, which may limit cross-modal learning.
   method:  ViLT incorporates whole word masking during pre-training (masking all wordpieces of a word at 0.15 probability) to force greater reliance on visual context, and applies RandAugment during fine-tuning (N=2, M=9; excluding inversion and cutout). Ablations show these choices consistently improve downstream scores across VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval compared to baselines without them.

7. [CV] ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer (2203.10790)
   problem: Designing a general-purpose vision backbone that balances accuracy and efficiency across classification, detection, and segmentation while modeling long-range dependencies without deep stacking.
   method:  ScalableViT is a hierarchical ViT backbone that alternates IW-MSA and SSA blocks within each stage and inserts a positional encoding generator (PEG) at the front of stages. This topology leverages SSA’s context-preserving scaling and IWSA’s inter-window interaction to capture long-range dependencies in single layers, improving the accuracy–cost trade-off. Empirically, ScalableViT-S improves ImageNet-1K top-1 by +1.4% over Twins-SVT-S and +1.8% over Swin-T at similar FLOPs, and yields substantial gains on COCO detection and ADE20K segmentation.

8. [CV] Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models (2401.08541)
   problem: Next-patch pixel prediction can force the backbone to specialize in low-level details, hurting the quality of transferable features.
   method:  Insert a heavily-parameterized token-level MLP prediction head atop the ViT trunk that processes each patch independently before projecting to pixels. This head captures low-level signals needed for accurate pixel regression, keeping the trunk focused on higher-level, transferable representations. Experiments show the MLP head significantly improves downstream performance over having no head, a transformer head brings only marginal gains at much higher cost, and increasing head depth (more MLP blocks) is especially beneficial.

9. [MULTIMODAL] Bridging Vision and Language Encoders: Parameter-Efficient Tuning for Referring Image Segmentation (2307.11545)
   problem: Referring image segmentation with pre-trained dual-encoder vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) needs strong cross-modal interaction and spatial inductive biases, but full fine-tuning is costly; keeping backbones frozen typically hurts performance.
   method:  Introduce Bridger, an adapter-like module inserted at intermediate stages of frozen image and text encoders to enable early, bidirectional interaction while injecting vision-specific inductive biases. Bridger comprises: (1) a Zoom Layer that reshapes visual features with convolution/deconvolution (for ViT and ResNet) to capture local spatial priors and control sequence length; and (2) an Interactor that performs cross-modal attention using one modality as query and the other as keys/values, then projects and residually adds outputs back into the next backbone stage. This design updates only 1.61%–3.38% of backbone parameters yet matches or surpasses full fine-tuning; ablations show removing Bridger drops oIoU by ~3.33%.

10. [IR_REC] Multi-Task Fusion via Reinforcement Learning for Long-Term User Satisfaction in Recommender Systems (2208.04560)
   problem: Evaluating new long-term optimization policies offline is risky because standard OPE can overestimate returns on out-of-distribution state-action pairs, potentially promoting harmful policies to online A/B tests.
   method:  Propose a Conservative Offline Policy Estimator that performs fitted Q evaluation augmented with a CQL-style regularizer to penalize Q-values for OOD actions, producing a lower-bound estimate of a policy’s per-session long-term value from held-out logs. This estimator guides offline model selection and aligns with observed online outcomes, filtering policies that would degrade user experience.

11. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

12. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Using options as policy building blocks can lead to suboptimal final behavior and sensitivity to the chosen option set; model-based rollouts can suffer from model bias.
   method:  Use skills only to guide exploration—not to constrain the learned policy—so the final policy remains over primitive actions. Empirically, this look-ahead exploration learns faster and reaches higher success rates than HER, parameterized action space (PAS), hierarchical HER-PAS, and parameter-space noise on Baxter manipulation tasks (pick-and-move, put-in, stack, take-out). The approach remains effective under model mismatch (e.g., an unseen wall) and with reduced skill sets, though learning slows when essential skills like grasp are missing.

13. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

14. [IR_REC] Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval (2203.08372)
   problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
   method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

15. [LLM_NLP] Pre-Training Transformers as Energy-Based Cloze Models (2012.08561)
   problem: Pseudo-log-likelihood (PLL) scoring for reranking text outputs (e.g., ASR/NMT n-best lists) is slow with masked LMs (n forward passes per sequence) and left-to-right LMs can underperform for reranking compared to bidirectional models.
   method:  Leverage Electric’s bidirectional, energy-based token scoring to compute PLLs for all tokens in a single transformer pass. Apply these PLLs to rerank 100-best ASR hypotheses on LibriSpeech via linear interpolation with the ASR score. Electric achieves lower WER than GPT-2 trained on comparable data and runs in 1 pass versus n for BERT, while being competitive with or better than ELECTRA-TT, demonstrating fast and effective reranking.

16. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
   method:  Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.

17. [SPEECH] DNCASR: End-to-End Training for Speaker-Attributed ASR (2506.01916)
   problem: Speaker-attributed ASR for long multi-party meetings needs consistent meeting-wide speaker indices and accurate attribution in overlapping speech, but prior integrated or parallel systems either require non-neural clustering or suffer from misalignment between ASR outputs and speaker index prediction.
   method:  Propose DNCASR, an end-to-end trainable architecture with two encoders (a waveform encoder for local token-level acoustic/lexical features and a speaker encoder for window-level speaker embeddings) and two linked decoders (an SOT ASR decoder and a neural clustering (DNC) decoder). A novel cross-attention link allows the DNC decoder to attend directly to the ASR decoder’s token-level hidden features (W_CA), aligning predicted speaker indices with the serialized word tokens and enabling joint optimization under a single loss. Compared to a parallel DNC+ASR baseline, DNCASR yields lower speaker-attributed error (e.g., AMI-MDM Eval cpWER 31.5% with CDA vs 34.6% parallel; 9.0% relative reduction) and larger gains on multi-talker segments.

18. [SPEECH] Unlocking Speech Instruction Data Potential with Query Rewriting (2507.08603)
   problem: Validating that TTS-synthesized speech preserves the linguistic content of the source text is unreliable with a single ASR or WER-only checks; ASR errors lead to improper filtering and dataset bias.
   method:  Use multi-agent annotation and verification: transcribe each synthesized audio with three diverse ASR models (whisper-large-v3, canary-1b, parakeet-tdt-1.1b) and compute semantic similarity to the original text using three embedding models (gte-large-en-v1.5, mxbai-embed-large-v1, stella_en_400M_v5). Average across ASRs and embeddings to form a SIM score and filter with a threshold (α=0.9). This ensemble reduces annotation errors and aligns well with WER (e.g., >98% consistency when WER=0) and improves WER over single-ASR selection (Table 4).

19. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Contact-rich manipulation is difficult to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and uncertainty; prior methods hard-code how to exploit environmental constraints rather than letting robots autonomously create constraints that funnel uncertainty and speed up learning.
   method:  Introduce a two-loop framework where an outer loop places a physical fixture to scaffold the task and an inner loop learns the manipulation policy. The outer loop observes a top-view depth image s_f0 and selects a fixture pose a_f, then receives as reward the inner loop’s cumulative return after a fixed number of episodes, incentivizing fixture placements that accelerate learning. It learns a CNN-based Q-function Q_w(s_f0, a_f) and uses the cross-entropy method at test time to select poses. The inner loop uses a vision-based RL policy (variant of A3C) from RGB images to end-effector motions executed via operational space control, yielding dramatically faster learning and higher success on peg insertion, wrench manipulation, and shallow-depth insertion.

20. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

21. [ROBOTICS] Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning (1708.04225)
   problem: Many manipulation tasks depend on multiple objects whose relative positions matter, requiring perception that can jointly localize several task-relevant items and feed a compact state to the policy.
   method:  Stack multiple attention vectors in W to attend to several objects simultaneously over the shared meta-attention proposals and semantic features. Learn these attentions from a small set of kinesthetic demonstrations (initialized with example crops), then feed the attended boxes to a guided policy search controller. Demonstrated on a sweeping task (orange into dustpan) with varying object positions, achieving strong performance and robustness to distractors.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

(empty on first round — issue your first search)
