messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
VLM inference latency scales with visual token count but most
tokens are redundant; how can we identify and remove or merge
low-information visual tokens at inference without retraining
the encoder?


### Proposed method
SCOPE-V: Spectral Certificate-On-demand Pruning and Expansion is a training-free, plug-in pipeline that replaces most visual tokens with a small, text-conditioned spectral basis plus a few leverage-score exemplars, and then adapts this basis online across early language layers with error certificates and on-demand unpruning. It avoids retraining the vision encoder and circumvents per-token K/V projections by aggregating pruned-token contributions in the pre-projection space.

Step 1 — Query-conditioned spectral condensation: From the frozen vision encoder, collect N tokens X ∈ R^{N×d} and 2D coordinates. Form a text summary vector t by averaging language-side queries at the first vision–language cross-attention call. Compute relevance weights w_i = softmax(λ · (x_i W_k · t)), and build a weighted, mean-centered matrix X_w. Run a fast Frequent-Directions-style sketch (or incremental PCA) to obtain a rank-r orthonormal basis U_r and singular values σ. Construct r spectral tokens s_j = σ_j u_j and keep m exemplar tokens with highest text-conditioned leverage l_i = ||(I − U_r U_r^T)x_i||^2 · w_i. For pruned tokens, compute nonnegative assignment coefficients to the spectral tokens via projection magnitudes a_{ij} = |x_i^T u_j| normalized across j.

Step 2 — Value-preserving aggregation via RBF barycentric routing: To avoid computing W_v and W_k on every pruned token, aggregate them before projection. Build r residual buckets S_j = ∑_{i pruned} π_{ij} x_i, where π_{ij} blends projection-based a_{ij} with a compact RBF kernel over 2D coordinates and feature cosine, ensuring locality. Only the r spectral tokens and m exemplars are passed through the VLM’s linear maps: K,V for spectral tokens are K(s_j), V(s_j) and a residual correction R_j = W_v S_j. Each spectral token’s value becomes Ṽ_j = V(s_j) + α · R_j, where α rescales mass to conserve the summed L1 norm of values from its assigned pruned tokens. Exemplar tokens pass through unchanged. This yields r + m tokens total, while the contributions of all other tokens are preserved linearly without per-token projections.

Step 3 — Discretely-online refinement with prototype-certified unpruning: Run the language model with the condensed set for a short layer interval (e.g., the first one or two cross-attn layers). After the interval, compute a deterministic, prototype-margin certificate over the spectral tokens: treat {s_j} as prototypes in the attended key space and measure, per language position, the entropy or top-2 cosine margin of its attention over prototypes. If uncertainty is high and attention gradients concentrate on a prototype p, on-demand unprune the top-b pruned tokens with largest residual energy toward p (e.g., highest π_{ip}·w_i or leverage). Update the spectral basis discretely-online by rerunning a small-rank Frequent Directions on X using new relevance weights proportional to the actually observed attention, smoothed by an uncertainty-aware EMA; keep r fixed and swap in any new directions with largest singular mass. Proceed in short intervals, repeating certificate→optional unpruning→basis refresh, until the token budget or a low-uncertainty state is reached.

Implementation notes: SCOPE-V is architecture-agnostic and needs only to (a) read the first cross-attn queries to form t, (b) replace the vision token list with {spectral tokens + exemplars}, and (c) inject on-demand tokens at layer boundaries. Complexity is O(N d r) once per image to form U_r and O((r + m) d) per head thereafter. Aggregation is K-nearest in the 2D grid, typically K ≤ 4, and does not require per-token K/V. The method degrades gracefully: if no text is available (captioning, OCR-free modes), it falls back to unconditional spectral condensation (w_i uniform); if the certificate remains high-entropy, the system expands toward the full set under the same interface.

### Rationale
The latency bottleneck in VLMs scales with visual token count, but most tokens lie close to a low-dimensional subspace conditioned on the current instruction. Rather than pruning/merging by clustering or attention heuristics, SCOPE-V constructs a text-conditioned spectral basis that linearly spans the information needed by the language queries and replaces thousands of tokens with r directions plus a handful of outlier exemplars. Because attention projections are linear, aggregating pruned-token contributions before the V map preserves their effect with a single projection per basis element, yielding large compute savings without retraining.

Two additional ingredients make the approach robust and novel. First, a deterministic, prototype-margin certificate over the spectral tokens detects when the condensed representation is ambiguous for the current textual queries, and triggers targeted, on-demand unpruning only where evidence is lacking. Second, the spectral basis is updated discretely-online across early language layers using the attention actually observed, with uncertainty-aware EMA smoothing, so the condensed representation tracks what the model needs as reasoning unfolds—without ever peeking ahead or modifying the frozen encoder. Together, these components provide an anytime, error-aware token reduction mechanism that operates entirely at inference time and is complementary to existing token-selection and merging schemes.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
   method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

5. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

6. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
   method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

7. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

8. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

9. [ROBOTICS] TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model (2509.10063)
   problem: Non-optical tactile sensors often trade off sensitivity for range (or vice versa), limiting their usefulness in manipulation that spans delicate touch to high-force contacts.
   method:  PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

10. [RL] Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters (1909.02119)
   problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
   method:  The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.

11. [MULTIMODAL] Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning (2412.02071)
   problem: Downstream video understanding and selection tasks lack temporally fine-grained supervision to pick informative frames and reason about ordered events.
   method:  Use ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame, progress-aware captions as an intermediate textual representation. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.

12. [ROBOTICS] Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting (2103.09555)
   problem: Robotics lacks a structured textile taxonomy aligned with industry practice, making it hard to study and benchmark methods for identifying fabric properties relevant to manipulation.
   method:  The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted). Textile classes are determined from microscope imagery of samples to capture construction details invisible to normal cameras. A dataset of 40 labeled samples is built according to this taxonomy, and analyses (t-SNE, SVM, CNN) show construction-based labeling yields clearer separability and substantially higher accuracy than material-only labels.

13. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
   method:  Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.

14. [CV] Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction (1912.12773)
   problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

17. [CV] Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection (2003.11818)
   problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
   method:  Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.

18. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
   method:  Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.

19. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Reasoning over videos with LVLMs is computationally expensive due to many redundant frames and long contexts, and models often lack structured, evidence-driven reasoning.
   method:  Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. A tiny LVLM (~1B) captions sampled frames and a tiny LLM (~0.5–1.5B) selects question-relevant core frames; the reasoning LVLM is trained with a two-stage SFT—stage-1 generates multimodal evidence and stage-2 answers using that evidence. At inference, the selector reduces inputs to around 2–3 core frames, then the LVLM performs CoT-based reasoning, yielding higher accuracy and substantially lower TFLOPs on a 14-task benchmark; the selector is plug-and-play and improves efficiency across different LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, InternVL2).

20. [CV] Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty (2207.10130)
   problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
   method:  Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses. This learns a discriminative latent space without enforcing Lipschitz constraints, enabling single forward-pass uncertainty estimation that scales to classification, semantic segmentation, and depth regression. An auxiliary MLP head g_unc operates on the DM embedding to output a confidence score. Experiments show competitive uncertainty performance with Deep Ensembles across tasks at much lower computational cost.

21. [LLM_NLP] MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation (2509.21978)
   problem: LLM-generated research ideas often lack rigorous motivational grounding in the literature due to probabilistic internal knowledge and limited context, leading to superficial or infeasible proposals.
   method:  Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes research motivations as (problem, challenge, solution) triples with explicit edge types (parent-of, problem–challenge, challenge–solution). Build it automatically via SciMotivMiner, which extracts standardized, author-agnostic triples from papers and merges duplicates, then add a hierarchy using a Parent Node Addition algorithm that embeds nodes, finds nearest neighbors, and leverages an LLM to form general parent nodes. During ideation, agents use graph tools (fuzzy node search and relation retrieval) to ground ideas on concrete nodes from the graph, supplying structured, cross-linked motivational context for each proposal.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

Last search results:
(empty on first round — issue your first search)

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).

Thought: I need to check if there are existing methods that combine spectral/PCA-based token compression with text-conditioning for VLM visual token reduction at inference time.
Query: spectral token compression visual language model inference training-free

Round 2/10.

1. DepthCache: Depth-Guided Training-Free Visual Token Merging for Vision-Language-Action Model Inference ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable generalist robotic manipulation but suffer from high inference latency. This bottleneck stems from the massive number of visual tokens processed by large language backbones. Existing methods either prune or merge tokens uniformly, degrading the spatial reasoning essential for robotic control. We present DepthCache, a training-free framework that leverages depth as a structural prior for visual token compression. It partitions observations into depth-based regions and applies spatially differentiated merge ratios, preserving the near-field workspace while compressing the distant background. To exploit temporal redundancy, DepthCache distributes the merging process across consecutive frames, ensuring consistent representations while reducing per-step computation. A motion-adaptive pipeline further optimizes auxiliary view compression based on end-effector dynamics. The framework requires no model modification, generalizing across diverse VLA architectures. On the LIBERO benchmark, DepthCache achieves up to 1.28x inference speedup with less than 1% average success rate degradation across three VLA models (pi_0.5, OpenVLA, GR00T), whereas pruning and merging baselines incur 4--24% degradation at comparable compression. Real-world experiments on a physical manipulator demonstrate that DepthCache enables faster task throughput and more responsive closed-loop control in latency-sensitive scenarios.

2. SparseVLM: Visual Token Sparsification for Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference (International Conference on Machine Learning 2024, cites: 308)
   In vision-language models (VLMs), visual tokens usually bear a significant amount of computational overhead despite sparsity of information in them when compared to text tokens. To address this, most existing methods learn a network to prune redundant visual tokens using certain training data. Differently, we propose a text-guided training-free token optimization mechanism dubbed SparseVLM that eliminates the need of extra parameters or fine-tuning costs. Given that visual tokens complement text tokens in VLM's linguistic reasoning, we select relevant text tokens to rate the significance of visual tokens using self-attention matrices and, then, prune visual tokens using the proposed strategy to maximize sparsity while retaining information. In particular, we introduce a rank-based strategy to adaptively determine the sparsification ratio for each layer, alongside a token recycling method that compresses pruned tokens into more compact representations. Experimental results show that SparseVLM increases the efficiency of various VLMs in a number of image and video understanding tasks. For example, LLaVA when equipped with SparseVLM achieves 54% reduction in FLOPs, 37% decrease in CUDA latency while maintaining 97% of its original accuracy. Our code is available at https://github.com/Gumpest/SparseVLMs.

3. TokenCarve: Information-Preserving Visual Token Compression in Multimodal Large Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 13)
   Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are becoming increasingly popular, while the high computational cost associated with multimodal data input, particularly from visual tokens, poses a significant challenge. Existing training-based token compression methods improve inference efficiency but require costly retraining, while training-free methods struggle to maintain performance when aggressively reducing token counts. In this study, we reveal that the performance degradation of MLLM closely correlates with the accelerated loss of information in the attention output matrix. This insight introduces a novel information-preserving perspective, making it possible to maintain performance even under extreme token compression. Based on this finding, we propose TokenCarve, a training-free, plug-and-play, two-stage token compression framework. The first stage employs an Information-Preservation-Guided Selection (IPGS) strategy to prune low-information tokens, while the second stage further leverages IPGS to guide token merging, minimizing information loss. Extensive experiments on 11 datasets and 2 model variants demonstrate the effectiveness of TokenCarve. It can even reduce the number of visual tokens to 22.2% of the original count, achieving a 1.23x speedup in inference, a 64% reduction in KV cache storage, and only a 1.54% drop in accuracy. Our code is available at https://github.com/ShawnTan86/TokenCarve.

4. OmniZip: Audio-Guided Dynamic Token Compression for Fast Omnimodal Large Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 11)
   Omnimodal large language models (OmniLLMs) have attracted increasing research attention of late towards unified audio-video understanding. However, the high computational cost of processing longer joint audio-video token sequences has become a key bottleneck. Existing token compression methods have not addressed the emerging need to jointly compress multimodal tokens. To bridge this gap, we present OmniZip, a training-free, audio-guided audio-visual token-compression framework that optimizes multimodal token representation and accelerates model inference. Specifically, OmniZip first identifies salient audio tokens, then computes an audio retention score for each time group to capture information density, thereby dynamically guiding video token pruning and preserving cues from audio anchors enhanced by cross-modal similarity. For each time window, OmniZip compresses the video tokens using an interleaved spatio-temporal scheme. Extensive results demonstrate the merits of OmniZip: it achieves a 3.42X inference speedup and a 1.4X memory reduction over other top-performing counterparts, while maintaining the performance of OmniLLMs without training.

5. Think Twice, Act Once: Token-Aware Compression and Action Reuse for Efficient Inference in Vision-Language-Action Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 19)
   Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for general-purpose robot control through natural language instructions. However, their high inference cost-stemming from large-scale token computation and autoregressive decoding-poses significant challenges for real-time deployment and edge applications. While prior work has primarily focused on architectural optimization, we take a different perspective by identifying a dual form of redundancy in VLA models: (i) high similarity across consecutive action steps, and (ii) substantial redundancy in visual tokens. Motivated by these observations, we propose FlashVLA, the first training-free and plug-and-play acceleration framework that enables action reuse in VLA models. FlashVLA improves inference efficiency through a token-aware action reuse mechanism that avoids redundant decoding across stable action steps, and an information-guided visual token selection strategy that prunes low-contribution tokens. Extensive experiments on the LIBERO benchmark show that FlashVLA reduces FLOPs by 55.7% and latency by 36.0%, with only a 0.7% drop in task success rate. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of FlashVLA in enabling lightweight, low-latency VLA inference without retraining.

6. Position-Aware Depth Decay Decoding (D3): Boosting Large Language Model Inference Efficiency (Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics 2025, cites: 2)
   Due to the large number of parameters, the inference phase of Large Language Models (LLMs) is resource-intensive. Unlike traditional model compression, which needs retraining, recent dynamic computation methods show that not all components are required for inference, enabling a training-free pipeline. In this paper, we focus on the dynamic depth of LLM generation. A token-position aware layer skipping framework is proposed to save 1.5x times operations efficiently while maintaining performance. We first observed that tokens predicted later have lower perplexity and thus require less computation. Then, we propose a training-free algorithm called Position-Aware Depth Decay Decoding ($D^3$), which leverages a power-law decay function, $\left\lfloor L \times (\alpha^i) \right\rfloor$, to determine the number of layers to retain when generating token $T_i$. Remarkably, without any retraining, the $D^3$ achieves success across a wide range of generation tasks for the first time. Experiments on large language models (\ie the Llama) with $7 \sim 70$ billion parameters show that $D^3$ can achieve an average 1.5x speedup compared with the full-inference pipeline while maintaining comparable performance with nearly no performance drop ($<1\%$) on the GSM8K and BBH benchmarks.

7. PromptDistill: Query-based Selective Token Retention in Intermediate Layers for Efficient Large Language Model Inference (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   As large language models (LLMs) tackle increasingly complex tasks and longer documents, their computational and memory costs during inference become a major bottleneck. To address this, we propose PromptDistill, a novel, training-free method that improves inference efficiency while preserving generation quality. PromptDistill identifies and retains the most informative tokens by leveraging attention interactions in early layers, preserving their hidden states while reducing the computational burden in later layers. This allows the model to focus on essential contextual information without fully processing all tokens. Unlike previous methods such as H2O and SnapKV, which perform compression only after processing the entire input, or GemFilter, which selects a fixed portion of the initial prompt without considering contextual dependencies, PromptDistill dynamically allocates computational resources to the most relevant tokens while maintaining a global awareness of the input. Experiments using our method and baseline approaches with base models such as LLaMA 3.1 8B Instruct, Phi 3.5 Mini Instruct, and Qwen2 7B Instruct on benchmarks including LongBench, InfBench, and Needle in a Haystack demonstrate that PromptDistill significantly improves efficiency while having minimal impact on output quality compared to the original models. With a single-stage selection strategy, PromptDistill effectively balances performance and efficiency, outperforming prior methods like GemFilter, H2O, and SnapKV due to its superior ability to retain essential information. Specifically, compared to GemFilter, PromptDistill achieves an overall $1\%$ to $5\%$ performance improvement while also offering better time efficiency. Additionally, we explore multi-stage selection, which further improves efficiency while maintaining strong generation performance.

8. PIO-FVLM: Rethinking Training-Free Visual Token Reduction for VLM Acceleration from an Inference-Objective Perspective (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Recently, reducing redundant visual tokens in vision-language models (VLMs) to accelerate VLM inference has emerged as a hot topic. However, most existing methods rely on heuristics constructed based on inter-visual-token similarity or cross-modal visual-text similarity, which gives rise to certain limitations in compression performance and practical deployment. In contrast, we propose PIO-FVLM from the perspective of inference objectives, which transforms visual token compression into preserving output result invariance and selects tokens primarily by their importance to this goal. Specially, vision tokens are reordered with the guidance of token-level gradient saliency generated by our designed layer-local proxy loss, a coarse constraint from the current layer to the final result. Then the most valuable vision tokens are selected following the non-maximum suppression (NMS) principle. The proposed PIO-FVLM is training-free and compatible with FlashAttention, friendly to practical application and deployment. It can be deployed independently as an encoder-free method, or combined with encoder compression approaches like VisionZip for use as an encoder-involved method. On LLaVA-Next-7B, PIO-FVLM retains just 11.1% of visual tokens but maintains 97.2% of the original performance, with a 2.67$\times$ prefill speedup, 2.11$\times$ inference speedup, 6.22$\times$ lower FLOPs, and 6.05$\times$ reduced KV Cache overhead. Our code is available at https://github.com/ocy1/PIO-FVLM.

9. Semantic-Geometric Dual Compression: Training-Free Visual Token Reduction for Ultra-High-Resolution Remote Sensing Understanding ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated immense potential in Earth observation. However, the massive visual tokens generated when processing Ultra-High-Resolution (UHR) imagery introduce prohibitive computational overhead, severely bottlenecking their inference efficiency. Existing visual token compression methods predominantly adopt static and uniform compression strategies, neglecting the inherent"Semantic-Geometric Duality"in remote sensing interpretation tasks. Specifically, object semantic tasks focus on the abstract semantics of objects and benefit from aggressive background pruning, whereas scene geometric tasks critically rely on the integrity of spatial topology. To address this challenge, we propose DualComp, a task-adaptive dual-stream token compression framework. Dynamically guided by a lightweight pre-trained router, DualComp decouples feature processing into two dedicated pathways. In the object semantic stream, the Spatially-Contiguous Semantic Aggregator (SCSA) utilizes size-adaptive clustering to aggregates redundant background while protecting small object. In the scene geometric stream, the Instruction-Guided Structure Recoverer (IGSR) introduces a greedy path-tracing topology completion mechanism to reconstruct spatial skeletons. Experiments on the UHR remote sensing benchmark XLRS-Bench demonstrate that DualComp accomplishes high-fidelity remote sensing interpretation at an exceptionally low computational cost, achieving simultaneous improvements in both efficiency and accuracy.

10. EfficientVLA: Training-Free Acceleration and Compression for Vision-Language-Action Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 51)
   Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, particularly diffusion-based architectures, demonstrate transformative potential for embodied intelligence but are severely hampered by high computational and memory demands stemming from extensive inherent and inference-time redundancies. While existing acceleration efforts often target isolated inefficiencies, such piecemeal solutions typically fail to holistically address the varied computational and memory bottlenecks across the entire VLA pipeline, thereby limiting practical deployability. We introduce EfficientVLA, a structured and training-free inference acceleration framework that systematically eliminates these barriers by cohesively exploiting multifaceted redundancies. EfficientVLA synergistically integrates three targeted strategies: (1) pruning of functionally inconsequential layers from the language module, guided by an analysis of inter-layer redundancies; (2) optimizing the visual processing pathway through a task-aware strategy that selects a compact, diverse set of visual tokens, balancing task-criticality with informational coverage; and (3) alleviating temporal computational redundancy within the iterative diffusion-based action head by strategically caching and reusing key intermediate features. We apply our method to a standard VLA model CogACT, yielding a 1.93X inference speedup and reduces FLOPs to 28.9%, with only a 0.6% success rate drop in the SIMPLER benchmark.

2. STAR: Stage-Wise Attention-Guided Token Reduction for Efficient Large Vision-Language Models Inference (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Although large vision-language models (LVLMs) leverage rich visual token representations to achieve strong performance on multimodal tasks, these tokens also introduce significant computational overhead during inference. Existing training-free token pruning methods typically adopt a single-stage strategy, focusing either on visual self-attention or visual-textual cross-attention. However, such localized perspectives often overlook the broader information flow across the model, leading to substantial performance degradation, especially under high pruning ratios. In this work, we propose STAR (Stage-wise Attention-guided token Reduction), a training-free, plug-and-play framework that approaches token pruning from a global perspective. Instead of pruning at a single point, STAR performs attention-guided reduction in two complementary stages: an early-stage pruning based on visual self-attention to remove redundant low-level features, and a later-stage pruning guided by cross-modal attention to discard task-irrelevant tokens. This holistic approach allows STAR to significantly reduce computational cost while better preserving task-critical information. Extensive experiments across multiple LVLM architectures and benchmarks show that STAR achieves strong acceleration while maintaining comparable, and in some cases even improved performance.

3. WSVD: Weighted Low-Rank Approximation for Fast and Efficient Execution of Low-Precision Vision-Language Models ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) has become an important technique for reducing the computational burden of Vision Language Models (VLMs), which play a central role in tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering. Although multiple prior works have proposed efficient SVD variants to enable low-rank operations, we find that in practice it remains difficult to achieve substantial latency reduction during model execution. To address this limitation, we introduce a new computational pattern and apply SVD at a finer granularity, enabling real and measurable improvements in execution latency. Furthermore, recognizing that weight elements differ in their relative importance, we adaptively allocate relative importance to each element during SVD process to better preserve accuracy, then extend this framework with quantization applied to both weights and activations, resulting in a highly efficient VLM. Collectively, we introduce~\textit{Weighted SVD} (WSVD), which outperforms other approaches by achieving over $1.8\times$ decoding speedup while preserving accuracy. We open source our code at: \href{https://github.com/SAI-Lab-NYU/WSVD}{\texttt{https://github.com/SAI-Lab-NYU/WSVD}

4. QSVD: Efficient Low-rank Approximation for Unified Query-Key-Value Weight Compression in Low-Precision Vision-Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 3)
   Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are integral to tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering, but their high computational cost, driven by large memory footprints and processing time, limits their scalability and real-time applicability. In this work, we propose leveraging Singular-Value Decomposition (SVD) over the joint query (Q), key (K), and value (V) weight matrices to reduce KV cache size and computational overhead. We in addition introduce an efficient rank allocation strategy that dynamically adjusts the SVD rank based on its impact on VLM accuracy, achieving a significant reduction in both memory usage and computational cost. Finally, we extend this approach by applying quantization to both VLM weights and activations, resulting in a highly efficient VLM. Our method outperforms previous approaches that rely solely on quantization or SVD by achieving more than $10\%$ accuracy improvement while consuming less hardware cost, making it better for real-time deployment on resource-constrained devices. We open source our code at \href{https://github.com/SAI-Lab-NYU/QSVD}{\texttt{https://github.com/SAI-Lab-NYU/QSVD}}.

5. CoVSpec: Efficient Device-Edge Co-Inference for Vision-Language Models via Speculative Decoding ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in multimodal perception and reasoning. However, deploying large VLMs on mobile devices remains challenging due to their substantial computational and memory demands. A practical alternative is device-edge co-inference, where a lightweight draft VLM on the mobile device collaborates with a larger target VLM on the edge server via speculative decoding. Nevertheless, directly extending speculative decoding to VLMs suffers from severe inefficiency due to excessive visual-token computation and high communication overhead. To address these challenges, we propose CoVSpec, an efficient collaborative speculative decoding framework for VLM inference. Specifically, we first develop a training-free visual token reduction framework that prunes redundant visual tokens on the mobile device by jointly considering query relevance, token activity, and low-rank dependency. Moreover, we design an adaptive drafting strategy that dynamically adjusts both the verification frequency and the draft length. In addition, we introduce a parallel branching mechanism with decoupled verification-correction to improve draft-side utilization during target-side verification and reduce correction-related transmission overhead. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that CoVSpec achieves up to 2.21x higher throughput than target-only inference and reduces communication overhead by more than 96% compared with baselines, without compromising task accuracy.

6. Delta-LLaVA: Base-then-Specialize Alignment for Token-Efficient Vision-Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) combine visual and textual representations to enable rich reasoning capabilities. However, the high computational cost of processing dense visual tokens remains a major bottleneck. A critical component in this pipeline is the visual projector, which bridges the vision encoder and the language model. Standard designs often employ a simple multi-layer perceptron for direct token mapping, but this approach scales poorly with high-resolution inputs, introducing significant redundancy. We present Delta-LLaVA, a token-efficient projector that employs a low-rank DeltaProjection to align multi-level vision features into a compact subspace before further interaction. On top of this base alignment, lightweight Transformer blocks act as specialization layers, capturing both global and local structure under constrained token budgets. Extensive experiments and ablations demonstrate that this base-then-specialize design yields consistent gains across multiple benchmarks with only 144 tokens, highlighting the importance of token formation prior to scaling interaction capacity. With Delta-LLaVA, inference throughput improves by up to 55%, while end-to-end training accelerates by nearly 4-5x in pretraining and over 1.5x in finetuning, highlighting the dual benefits of our design in both efficiency and scalability.

7. HAWAII: Hierarchical Visual Knowledge Transfer for Efficient Vision-Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   Improving the visual understanding ability of vision-language models (VLMs) is crucial for enhancing their performance across various tasks. While using multiple pretrained visual experts has shown great promise, it often incurs significant computational costs during training and inference. To address this challenge, we propose HAWAII, a novel framework that distills knowledge from multiple visual experts into a single vision encoder, enabling it to inherit the complementary strengths of several experts with minimal computational overhead. To mitigate conflicts among different teachers and switch between different teacher-specific knowledge, instead of using a fixed set of adapters for multiple teachers, we propose to use teacher-specific Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters with a corresponding router. Each adapter is aligned with a specific teacher, avoiding noisy guidance during distillation. To enable efficient knowledge distillation, we propose fine-grained and coarse-grained distillation. At the fine-grained level, token importance scores are employed to emphasize the most informative tokens from each teacher adaptively. At the coarse-grained level, we summarize the knowledge from multiple teachers and transfer it to the student using a set of general-knowledge LoRA adapters with a router. Extensive experiments on various vision-language tasks demonstrate the superiority of HAWAII compared to popular open-source VLMs. The code is available at https://github.com/yimuwangcs/wise-hawaii.

8. Adaptive Rank Pruning: Dynamic Low-Rank Model Merging and Compression for Efficient AI Deployment (IEEE Access 2025, cites: 0)
   Deploying large, pretrained models on resource limited devices remains a fundamental challenge in machine learning. While model merging and low-rank compression represent two common options, they generally employ static approaches such as factorization with a fixed rank (e.g., singular value decomposition) or weight averaging, producing some degradation in performance. This work introduces Adaptive Rank Pruning (ARP), a dynamic, layer-wise optimization of the rank during merging by using a variance-thresholding criterion, creating a unified high quality approach to compression and merging. ARP does not require retraining and is evaluated through rigorous comparisons on both classical methods (SVD) and modern state-of-the-art baselines (LoRA and QLoRA). Extensive experiments on vision (ResNet) and language (BERT) tasks show that ARP achieves a better accuracy–compression trade-off ratio, producing up to $2.5\times $ model size reduction with less than 4% accuracy loss. We further demonstrate ARP on edge hardware (Raspberry Pi 4, Google Pixel 6), validating its ability to reduce inference latency and energy consumption compared to alternative methods. Our results reveal ARP as a robust and effective approach for deploying adaptable AI in real-world constrained environments.

9. Beyond Attention Magnitude: Leveraging Inter-layer Rank Consistency for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Models ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models excel in robotic manipulation but suffer from significant inference latency due to processing dense visual tokens. Existing token reduction methods predominantly rely on attention magnitude as a static selection. In this work, we challenge this assumption, revealing that high-attention tokens are task-dependent and can even degrade policy performance. To address this, we introduce \textbf{TIES} (\textbf{T}au-guided \textbf{I}nter-layer \textbf{E}fficient \textbf{S}election), a dynamic framework guided by inter-layer token ranking consistency. By adaptively balancing attention magnitude with ranking consistency, TIES ensures robust token selection without requiring additional training. On the CogACT + SIMPLER benchmark, TIES improves average success rates by 6\% while reducing token usage by 78\%, and demonstrate strong generalization across diverse decoders and benchmarks.

   Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success in multi-modal reasoning, but their inference time efficiency remains a significant challenge due to the memory overhead during decoding, especially when the query and answer of VLMs consist of long sequences of visual and text tokens. This paper presents AttentionPack, an adaptive and attention-aware optimization framework tailored for large vision-language models with improving memory-efficiency during decoding, focusing on addressing the challenges due to the increased high number of visual inputs and interactions, particularly in long-context tasks with multiple high-resolution images or videos. AttentionPack is novel in two aspects: (i) We introduce a multi-head attention compaction method for economically storing key and value matrices by exploiting the implicit low-rank structure, and (ii) we develop a token-specific attention-aware decompression mechanism to reduce latency overhead. Experimental results on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that AttentionPack improves memory efficiency by up to 8x, enabling higher batch sizes and faster batch inference while preserving the model output quality or longer context lengths for superior retrieval performance. You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
VLM inference latency scales with visual token count but most
tokens are redundant; how can we identify and remove or merge
low-information visual tokens at inference without retraining
the encoder?


### Proposed method
SCOPE-V: Spectral Certificate-On-demand Pruning and Expansion is a training-free, plug-in pipeline that replaces most visual tokens with a small, text-conditioned spectral basis plus a few leverage-score exemplars, and then adapts this basis online across early language layers with error certificates and on-demand unpruning. It avoids retraining the vision encoder and circumvents per-token K/V projections by aggregating pruned-token contributions in the pre-projection space.

Step 1 — Query-conditioned spectral condensation: From the frozen vision encoder, collect N tokens X ∈ R^{N×d} and 2D coordinates. Form a text summary vector t by averaging language-side queries at the first vision–language cross-attention call. Compute relevance weights w_i = softmax(λ · (x_i W_k · t)), and build a weighted, mean-centered matrix X_w. Run a fast Frequent-Directions-style sketch (or incremental PCA) to obtain a rank-r orthonormal basis U_r and singular values σ. Construct r spectral tokens s_j = σ_j u_j and keep m exemplar tokens with highest text-conditioned leverage l_i = ||(I − U_r U_r^T)x_i||^2 · w_i. For pruned tokens, compute nonnegative assignment coefficients to the spectral tokens via projection magnitudes a_{ij} = |x_i^T u_j| normalized across j.

Step 2 — Value-preserving aggregation via RBF barycentric routing: To avoid computing W_v and W_k on every pruned token, aggregate them before projection. Build r residual buckets S_j = ∑_{i pruned} π_{ij} x_i, where π_{ij} blends projection-based a_{ij} with a compact RBF kernel over 2D coordinates and feature cosine, ensuring locality. Only the r spectral tokens and m exemplars are passed through the VLM’s linear maps: K,V for spectral tokens are K(s_j), V(s_j) and a residual correction R_j = W_v S_j. Each spectral token’s value becomes Ṽ_j = V(s_j) + α · R_j, where α rescales mass to conserve the summed L1 norm of values from its assigned pruned tokens. Exemplar tokens pass through unchanged. This yields r + m tokens total, while the contributions of all other tokens are preserved linearly without per-token projections.

Step 3 — Discretely-online refinement with prototype-certified unpruning: Run the language model with the condensed set for a short layer interval (e.g., the first one or two cross-attn layers). After the interval, compute a deterministic, prototype-margin certificate over the spectral tokens: treat {s_j} as prototypes in the attended key space and measure, per language position, the entropy or top-2 cosine margin of its attention over prototypes. If uncertainty is high and attention gradients concentrate on a prototype p, on-demand unprune the top-b pruned tokens with largest residual energy toward p (e.g., highest π_{ip}·w_i or leverage). Update the spectral basis discretely-online by rerunning a small-rank Frequent Directions on X using new relevance weights proportional to the actually observed attention, smoothed by an uncertainty-aware EMA; keep r fixed and swap in any new directions with largest singular mass. Proceed in short intervals, repeating certificate→optional unpruning→basis refresh, until the token budget or a low-uncertainty state is reached.

Implementation notes: SCOPE-V is architecture-agnostic and needs only to (a) read the first cross-attn queries to form t, (b) replace the vision token list with {spectral tokens + exemplars}, and (c) inject on-demand tokens at layer boundaries. Complexity is O(N d r) once per image to form U_r and O((r + m) d) per head thereafter. Aggregation is K-nearest in the 2D grid, typically K ≤ 4, and does not require per-token K/V. The method degrades gracefully: if no text is available (captioning, OCR-free modes), it falls back to unconditional spectral condensation (w_i uniform); if the certificate remains high-entropy, the system expands toward the full set under the same interface.

### Rationale
The latency bottleneck in VLMs scales with visual token count, but most tokens lie close to a low-dimensional subspace conditioned on the current instruction. Rather than pruning/merging by clustering or attention heuristics, SCOPE-V constructs a text-conditioned spectral basis that linearly spans the information needed by the language queries and replaces thousands of tokens with r directions plus a handful of outlier exemplars. Because attention projections are linear, aggregating pruned-token contributions before the V map preserves their effect with a single projection per basis element, yielding large compute savings without retraining.

Two additional ingredients make the approach robust and novel. First, a deterministic, prototype-margin certificate over the spectral tokens detects when the condensed representation is ambiguous for the current textual queries, and triggers targeted, on-demand unpruning only where evidence is lacking. Second, the spectral basis is updated discretely-online across early language layers using the attention actually observed, with uncertainty-aware EMA smoothing, so the condensed representation tracks what the model needs as reasoning unfolds—without ever peeking ahead or modifying the frozen encoder. Together, these components provide an anytime, error-aware token reduction mechanism that operates entirely at inference time and is complementary to existing token-selection and merging schemes.

1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.
